title: Rethinking Retirement: Will Trump's Plan Bridge the Savings Gap?
content:
There are real policy chops behind the proposed Trump Savings Plan, which arrives as policy wonks are rethinking a broken retirement system. Millions of workers have no retirement wealth simply because they lack access to payroll-based savings. This lack of access maps directly onto the persistent problem of income inequality in the country. Trump's action won't completely resolve the retirement crisis, but it aims to help reduce this persistent coverage gap.

This type of plan has actually been tried before, during the Obama administration. In 2015, the government launched MyRA, a similar program that introduced voluntary benefits to those without workplace accounts. After two years, only 30,000 people joined, and the program was abruptly ended in 2017. The program failed primarily because Congress did not approve auto-enrollment, which is crucial for participation.

Research has conclusively shown that what makes 401(k) accounts successful is automatic enrollment. This mechanism supersedes the greatest obstacle to savings: simple inertia. However, implementing auto-enrollment on a national scale requires legislation from Congress. While there is great potential for the federal government to help struggling Americans, a simple White House announcement may not be enough.
